question: How is the Simple Past of regular verbs formed?
options: Add -ed to the base form: walk → walked, play → played, want → wanted. Spelling rules apply.
explanation: Regular Simple Past: base + -ed. Spelling: double consonant after short vowel (stop → stopped). Drop -e before -ed (like → liked). Change -y to -ied (study → studied). Same form for all persons.

question: How do you form the negative in Simple Past?
options: Use did not (didn't) + base form: She didn't go. He didn't know.
explanation: Negative: subject + did not + base form. "She didn't come." NOT "She didn't came." Did = auxiliary, so main verb stays in base form. Exception: be → was/were not (no did).

question: How do you form questions in Simple Past?
options: Did + subject + base form: Did she go? Did you see it?
explanation: Simple Past questions: Did + subject + base form. "Did he call?" NOT "Did he called?" Exception: be → Was/Were + subject. "Was she there?" Short answers: Yes, she did. / No, she didn't.

question: What is the Simple Past of "go"?
options: Went – irregular verb. Other forms: come → came, see → saw, have → had, be → was/were.
explanation: Irregular verbs: go→went, come→came, see→saw, say→said, get→got, make→made, know→knew, think→thought, take→took, give→gave. Must be learned by heart.

question: When do we use the Simple Past?
options: For finished actions in the past at a specific time. Signal words: yesterday, last week, in 2010, ago, when.
explanation: Simple Past: completed past action. "I visited Paris last year." Signal words: yesterday, last (night/week/year), in 2020, two days ago, when I was young. Compare: Present Perfect (no specific time mentioned).

question: What are the forms of "to be" in Simple Past?
options: I/he/she/it was; you/we/they were. Question: Was I? Were you? Negative: I wasn't; you weren't.
explanation: Be: was (singular: I, he, she, it) / were (plural: you, we, they). "I was happy." "They were late." Questions: "Was he there?" "Were you ready?" Negative: wasn't / weren't.

question: What is the Past Progressive (Past Continuous)?
options: Action in progress at a specific time in the past: was/were + verb-ing. Often combined with Simple Past: While I was reading, the phone rang.
explanation: Past Progressive: was/were + -ing. "I was sleeping at 10 pm." Interrupted action: "While I was cooking, she called." Contrast: Simple Past (short completed) vs. Past Progressive (longer background action).

question: How is the Present Perfect formed?
options: have/has + past participle. I have seen, she has gone, we have finished.
explanation: Present Perfect: have/has + past participle (3rd form). "I have eaten." "She has visited." Past participle: regular (-ed) or irregular (go→gone, see→seen, eat→eaten). No specific time signal.

question: What is the difference between Simple Past and Present Perfect?
options: Simple Past: finished action at specific time (yesterday). Present Perfect: past action with present relevance or no specific time (ever, never, already, just, yet).
Present Perfect is for recent actions only
explanation: SP: "I saw her yesterday." (specific time). PP: "I have seen her before." (no specific time, experience). Signal words: SP (yesterday, last year, ago, in 2010); PP (ever, never, already, just, yet, since, for).

question: What are comparative and superlative forms of adjectives?
options: Short adjectives: add -er (comparative) and -est (superlative): big→bigger→biggest. Long adjectives: more/most: beautiful→more beautiful→most beautiful.
explanation: Short (1-2 syllables): -er/-est: tall→taller→tallest. Spelling: double consonant (big→bigger), -y→-ier (happy→happier). Long: more/most. Irregular: good→better→best, bad→worse→worst, far→further→furthest.

question: What is an if-clause type 1?
options: Real or possible condition in the present/future: If + Simple Present, will + base form. If it rains, I will stay home.
explanation: Type 1 conditional: possible situation. "If I study hard, I will pass the exam." If-clause: Simple Present. Main clause: will + base form. Can use other modals: "If it rains, we can/might/should stay inside."

question: What is the difference between "much" and "many"?
options: Much: uncountable nouns (much water, much time). Many: countable nouns (many books, many people).
explanation: Much + uncountable: much money, much information, much homework. Many + countable (plural): many cars, many students, many ideas. In positive sentences: a lot of (both). Much/many mainly in negatives/questions.

question: What does "used to" express?
options: Past habit or state that no longer exists: I used to live in Berlin (but I don't anymore). Used to + base form.
explanation: "Used to": past habit/state, no longer true now. "I used to play football." (but I don't anymore). Questions: "Did you use to...?" Negative: "I didn't use to..." Compare: "would" (past habit, not state).

question: What are modal verbs in English?
options: Auxiliary verbs expressing ability, possibility, permission, obligation: can, could, may, might, must, should, will, would, shall, ought to.
explanation: Modals: always + base form (no -s, no -ed). Can (ability/permission): I can swim. Must (obligation): You must stop. Should (advice): You should eat well. May/might (possibility). Could (past ability/polite request).

question: What is the difference between "can" and "could"?
options: Can: present ability or permission. Could: past ability, polite request, or possibility: I could swim when I was five. Could you help me?
explanation: Can: present (I can speak German). Could: past ability (I could run fast as a child). Polite request: "Could you open the door?" Possibility: "It could rain." Could = more polite/tentative than can.

question: What is the passive voice in English?
options: Object becomes subject; formed with be + past participle: The book was written by her. Used when the doer is unknown or unimportant.
explanation: Passive: be + past participle. Active: "They built the bridge." Passive: "The bridge was built (by them)." Tenses: is written (present), was written (past), has been written (PP), will be written (future).

question: What are relative clauses in English?
options: Clauses that describe a noun: who (persons), which (things), that (persons/things). The man who lives there is my uncle.
explanation: Relative pronouns: who (people), which (things), that (both, informal). "The book that/which I read was great." "The woman who/that called is my teacher." Whose (possession): "The student whose bag I found..."

question: What is the gerund in English?
options: Verb used as a noun (verb + -ing as subject or object): Swimming is fun. I enjoy reading.
explanation: Gerund: verb-ing as noun. Subject: "Swimming is healthy." After certain verbs: enjoy, avoid, mind, finish, suggest, keep + gerund. "I enjoy swimming." After prepositions: "I'm good at cooking."

question: What is the infinitive with "to" used for?
options: After certain verbs (want, like, decide, hope, plan, need), after adjectives (happy to see you), or to express purpose (I went there to buy milk).
explanation: To + infinitive: want to go, decide to stay, hope to win. Purpose: "I study to learn." After adjectives: "glad to help." Modal verbs: no to (can do, must go, should eat). Gerund vs. infinitive: different verbs.

question: What does "since" vs "for" express with Present Perfect?
options: Since: point in time (since 2020, since Monday). For: duration of time (for three years, for a long time).
explanation: "I have lived here since 2015." (point of start). "I have lived here for 8 years." (duration). Since + point: Monday, January, 2010, I was a child. For + period: two hours, a long time, ages, three weeks.

question: What are the most common irregular verbs students should know?
options: All verbs are regular in English
go/went/gone, come/came/come, see/saw/seen, do/did/done, have/had/had, say/said/said, get/got/got, make/made/made, know/knew/known, take/took/taken.
explanation: Must-know irregulars: be/was/were/been, have/had/had, do/did/done, go/went/gone, come/came/come, see/saw/seen, give/gave/given, take/took/taken, write/wrote/written, speak/spoke/spoken.

question: What is reported speech (indirect speech)?
options: Reporting what someone said without using their exact words: She said (that) she was tired. Tense usually shifts back.
explanation: Reported speech: "I am tired" → She said (that) she was tired. Tense shift: present→past, past→past perfect, will→would. Pronouns change. Time/place expressions change (here→there, now→then, today→that day).

question: What is an adverb of frequency?
options: Word telling how often: always (100%), usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never (0%). Usually before the main verb but after "be".
explanation: Frequency adverbs: always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, seldom, never. Position: before main verb ("I always eat breakfast"), after "be" ("She is always late"). "Do you ever...?" "I have never been..."

question: What is the structure of a question with a question word?
options: Question word + auxiliary + subject + base form: Where did you go? What have you eaten? Why is she crying?
explanation: Wh-questions: Who, What, Where, When, Why, How + auxiliary + subject + base form. "Where did you go?" "What does she want?" Exception: who/what as subject: "Who called?" (no auxiliary needed).

question: What does the Present Perfect express that is different from Simple Past?
options: Present Perfect: connection to the present – experience ever/never, recent action (just), incomplete action (yet/still), action started in past and continues (since/for).
Present Perfect is always more polite
explanation: PP relevance: "I have lost my key." (it's still lost, affects now). SP: "I lost my key yesterday." (completed fact). Experience: "Have you ever been to Italy?" Action just completed: "She has just finished." Unfinished: "I have lived here for 5 years."

question: What is the difference between "a" and "an"?
options: A for countable, an for uncountable
A before consonant sounds: a car, a university. An before vowel sounds: an apple, an hour (h is silent), an MBA.
A for singular, an for plural
explanation: a/an = indefinite article. a + consonant sound: a book, a European (sounds like "Yuropean"). an + vowel sound: an apple, an elephant, an hour, an honest man. It is about the sound, not the spelling.

question: What are prepositions of time in English?
options: At (specific time: at 5pm, at noon, at night), on (days/dates: on Monday, on 3rd May), in (months/years/seasons: in July, in 2020, in summer).
explanation: Time prepositions: at (exact time: at 3pm, at midnight, at Christmas). on (specific day/date: on Monday, on my birthday, on 5th June). in (longer period: in the morning, in July, in 2020, in winter).

question: What are prepositions of place in English?
options: In (inside: in the box), on (surface: on the table), at (specific point: at the door, at school), under, above, next to, between, in front of, behind.
explanation: Place: in (3D space: in the room), on (surface: on the shelf), at (point/place: at home, at the bus stop). Under/below, over/above, next to, beside, between (two things), among (many things).

question: What is the difference between "make" and "do" in English?
options: Make: create/produce something (make a cake, make a decision, make a mistake). Do: perform an activity (do homework, do exercise, do the dishes).
Do is for physical actions only
explanation: Make: something new is created. Make a phone call, make dinner, make friends, make a plan. Do: activity without producing something tangible. Do sport, do business, do a favour, do your best. Fixed expressions!

question: What is "there is / there are"?
options: Used to say something exists: There is (singular/uncountable), there are (plural). There is a cat. There are three cats.
explanation: "There is/are" = existential. There is a problem. (singular). There are many people. (plural). Past: There was/were. Question: Is there...? Are there...? Negative: There isn't/aren't. Used to introduce new info.

question: What are the most important time connectors in English?
options: First, then, after that, finally, when, while, before, after, as soon as, until, during – connect events in time.
explanation: Time connectors: First → Then → After that → Finally (sequence). When (at the same time/after). While (simultaneous). Before/After (order). As soon as (immediately). Until (up to a point). During (throughout).

question: What is the rule for spelling -ing forms?
options: Double final consonant after short stressed vowel (swim→swimming, run→running). Drop silent -e (ride→riding). Change -ie to -y (lie→lying).
explanation: -ing spelling: simple: walk→walking, play→playing. Double consonant: short stressed vowel + single consonant: sit→sitting, stop→stopping. Drop -e: make→making, write→writing. -ie→-y: lie→lying, die→dying.

question: What are "false friends" between German and English?
options: Words that look similar but have different meanings: gift (Englisch: Geschenk, Deutsch: Gift=poison), bekommen (German=receive, not become), chef (English=Küchenchef, German=boss).
explanation: False friends: Gift (EN: present, DE: Geschenk/Gift=Poison). Become (EN: werden, DE: bekommen=receive). Sympathetic (EN: mitfühlend, DE: sympathisch=likeable). Art (EN: Kunst, DE: Art=type). Chef (EN: Küchenchef, DE: Chef=boss).

question: What is the imperative in English?
options: Use base form of verb for commands: Go! Stop! Listen carefully! For polite requests, add please: Please sit down. Negative: Don't + base form.
explanation: Imperative: base form, no subject. "Open the window." "Don't run." Polite: "Please be quiet." "Could you...?" The subject (you) is understood. For emphasis: "Do sit down!" (emphatic do).

question: What are the main differences between British and American English?
options: Spelling (colour/color, centre/center), vocabulary (lift/elevator, flat/apartment, autumn/fall), pronunciation, some grammar differences.
explanation: British vs American: Spelling: -our/-or (colour/color), -re/-er (centre/center), -ise/-ize. Vocab: lorry/truck, biscuit/cookie, chemist/drugstore, post/mail. Present Perfect (BrE) vs Simple Past (AmE) for recent actions.

question: What does "both ... and", "either ... or", "neither ... nor" express?
options: Both...and: two things included. Either...or: one of two choices. Neither...nor: both excluded.
explanation: Correlative conjunctions: Both A and B (A und B). Either A or B (entweder A oder B). Neither A nor B (weder A noch B). "Both cats and dogs are popular pets." "Either you study or you fail." "Neither she nor I knew."

question: What is a phrasal verb?
options: A verb + particle (preposition/adverb) with a new meaning: give up (aufgeben), look after (kümmern), turn off (ausschalten), find out (herausfinden).
explanation: Phrasal verbs: verb + particle = new meaning. Give up (quit), look forward to (sich freuen auf), put off (verschieben), get on with (auskommen), run out of (ausgehen). Very common in English, must be learned.

question: What is the difference between "say" and "tell"?
options: Say: used with direct/reported speech without naming the person spoken to: She said it was cold. Tell: always needs an object (person): She told me it was cold.
explanation: "Say" + (that) + clause: He said (that) he was tired. "Tell" + person + (that) + clause: He told me (that) he was tired. Tell sb: tell me, tell him, tell the class. Common mistake: "He said me" (wrong) → "He told me" (correct).

question: What is the difference between "look", "see", and "watch"?
options: See: without effort (I can see a bird). Look (at): directed gaze with effort. Watch: follow movement over time (watch TV, watch a game).
explanation: See: involuntary or ability (I see a bird, can you see it?). Look (at): deliberate direction of gaze (look at the map). Watch: follow a moving thing over time (watch the film, watch the children play). Similar: hear/listen to.

question: What are articles (definite and indefinite) in English?
options: A/an (indefinite): introduces something new or unknown. The (definite): refers to something specific or already mentioned. No article: plural/uncountable in general sense (I like cats).
explanation: A/an: first mention ("I saw a dog."). The: second mention or unique thing ("The dog was huge.", "the sun", "the Queen"). No article: general statements ("Dogs are friendly.", "I love music."). Tricky: "the" with superlatives, "the Rhine".
